event: The Betrayal of Jesus by Judas in the Garden of Gethsemane
symbols: kiss — perverted sign of love used as instrument of betrayal
halo around Jesus — divine identity and sinlessness even in humiliation
halo on woman at right — sanctity of the faithful who remain near Christ
torch and weapons — worldly power arrayed against the Prince of Peace
darkness — spiritual blindness and the 'hour of the power of darkness' (Luke 22:53)
Judas's embrace — counterfeit intimacy masking mortal treachery
Christ's calm posture — willing submission to the Father's redemptive plan
description: A sepia-toned composition shows Judas in the foreground embracing and kissing Jesus, who stands with a serene expression and a faint halo. Behind Judas, armed men with torches and weapons press forward from the left. To the right of Jesus, several disciples look on with distress, and a haloed woman — likely representing a holy follower — stands at the far right with a grieving expression. The figures are densely grouped, creating an atmosphere of urgency and encroaching violence around the central pair.
theological_description: The Kiss of Judas is the hinge-moment of the Passion narrative, where intimate discipleship collapses into the darkest betrayal in salvation history. Judas's kiss perverts the sign of fellowship (Romans 16:16) into a signal for arrest, fulfilling Jesus's own prophecy at the Last Supper (John 13:21-26). Christ's calm acceptance demonstrates His willing obedience to the Father's will (John 18:4-11; Matthew 26:54), transforming a moment of human treachery into the catalyst for cosmic redemption. The armed mob represents the futility of worldly force against the Lamb of God who lays down His life voluntarily. The grieving disciples embody the scattering prophesied in Zechariah 13:7 ('Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered'). Typologically, Judas's betrayal of a covenant brother echoes Joseph's brothers selling him (Genesis 37:28) and Joab's treacherous kiss of Amasa (2 Samuel 20:9-10), while Christ stands as the ultimate innocent sufferer foreshadowed throughout the Psalms.

scripture_refs.reason: Judas's prearranged sign — 'The one I kiss is the man; arrest him' — is the exact moment depicted
Jesus's poignant question: 'Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss?'
John's account of the arrest with lanterns, torches, and weapons matches the armed mob depicted
The striking of the shepherd and scattering of the flock — fulfilled in the disciples' distress and flight
Joab's treacherous kiss of Amasa prefigures Judas's use of a kiss as instrument of death
The close friend who shared bread lifting his heel — messianic psalm applied to Judas's betrayal (John 13:18)
Jesus declares 'this is your hour — when darkness reigns,' reflected in the dark tonal atmosphere
Joseph sold by his own brothers for silver prefigures Christ betrayed by His own disciple for thirty pieces
